# Secure GitHub Actions secrets with HashiCorp Vault

GitHub Actions has native OIDC token issuance built into the platform. Jobs can
request a signed JWT from GitHub's OIDC provider when the workflow grants
`id-token: write` permission. The token contains claims that identify what is
running, including the repository, branch or tag reference, deployment
environment, and workflow file.

<VideoEmbed url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lsWOx9bzAwY"/>

## Architecture

The action-to-Vault authentication flow is based on GitHub-issued OIDC tokens:

1. Runner requests a JWT from GitHub's OIDC provider (`token.actions.githubusercontent.com`) — requires `id-token: write` declared on the job
1. GitHub issues a JWT containing claims for the repository, branch, environment, and workflow file
1. Runner sends the JWT to Vault's JWT auth endpoint
1. Vault fetches signing keys from GitHub's shared OIDC discovery URL and validates the token signature
1. Vault evaluates `bound_claims` — repository, environment, ref, or `job_workflow_ref` — against the configured role
1. Vault issues a short-lived token scoped to the policies attached to the matching role

The runner uses the Vault token to retrieve the secrets authorized for that role.

![Diagram showing OIDC authentication flow between GitHub Actions and Vault](/img/well-architected-framework/diagram-cicd-github-actions-auth.png#light-theme-only)
![Diagram showing OIDC authentication flow between GitHub Actions and Vault](/img/well-architected-framework/diagram-cicd-github-actions-auth-dark.png#dark-theme-only)

### Runner hosting and auth method selection

The right auth method depends on where GitHub Actions runners execute. JWT/OIDC
auth works for all runner types because GitHub issues an OIDC token for any job
with `id-token: write` permission, regardless of the underlying infrastructure.
However, when jobs run on infrastructure you control, additional auth methods
become available that can provide stronger identity guarantees or remove the
dependency on reaching GitHub's OIDC endpoint.

- **GitHub-hosted runners** — JWT/OIDC is the only practical option. You do
  not control the underlying infrastructure, so there is no instance or pod
  identity to leverage.
- **Self-hosted runners on Kubernetes (Actions Runner Controller)** — Kubernetes
  auth is available in addition to JWT. The runner pod's service account token
  is verified directly by the cluster API, independent of GitHub.
- **Self-hosted runners on AWS, Azure, or GCP** — Cloud provider auth methods
  (AWS IAM, Azure Managed Identity, GCP IAM) are available. The runner instance
  already has a platform identity that Vault can validate against the cloud
  provider's API — no credential to provision or rotate.
- **Self-hosted runners on bare metal or air-gapped environments** — AppRole
  with response wrapping, or TLS certificate auth for persistent runner fleets.
  In air-gapped environments where GitHub's OIDC endpoint
  (`token.actions.githubusercontent.com`) is unreachable, AppRole becomes the
  required fallback.

## Authentication options

Because GitHub Actions has native OIDC token issuance, use JWT/OIDC auth for all
GitHub-hosted runners and internet-connected self-hosted runners. GitHub's
per-job OIDC tokens are the purpose-built mechanism for the Vault integration.
The tokens provide the following benefits:

- No long-lived, static credentials
- Fine-grained `bound_claims` that can restrict authentication to a specific
  repository, branch, deployment environment, or workflow file

The [hashicorp/vault-action](https://github.com/hashicorp/vault-action) GitHub
Action wraps the OIDC exchange and secret retrieval into a single workflow step.
The security controls live
on the Vault side, in how roles bind to GitHub's JWT claims — not in GitHub
itself.

Depending on your use case, there are additional auth methods that may be
appropriate for GitHub Actions workflows in your environment. For example,
if using self-hosted runners on Kubernetes managed by Actions Runner Controller,
use the Kubernetes auth method. The pod's service account token is more
authoritative than the GitHub JWT in that context and doesn't require the runner
to reach GitHub's OIDC endpoint.

The following table summarizes when each auth method fits best.

| Auth method | Security posture | Credential lifecycle | Best suited for |
|---|---|---|---|
| JWT / OIDC | High; per-job identity bound to GitHub repository, environment, and workflow metadata | Token issued per job by GitHub, no rotation needed | GitHub-hosted runners and internet-connected self-hosted runners |
| Cloud provider auth methods | Highest for cloud-hosted runners; identity is the platform infrastructure itself | Managed by cloud provider; no rotation required | Self-hosted runners on EC2, Azure VMs, or GCE instances with an assigned instance identity |
| Kubernetes | Highest for in-cluster; service account token verified directly by the cluster API | Short-lived projected service account token, auto-rotated by Kubernetes | Self-hosted runners managed by Actions Runner Controller on Kubernetes |
| TLS Certificate | High; mutual authentication | Certificate with defined expiry; requires rotation process | Long-lived, persistent runner fleets where mTLS is part of the existing security posture |
| AppRole | Moderate; requires secure secret ID delivery | Secret ID is single-use with response wrapping; RoleID is long-lived | Air-gapped self-hosted runners where neither cloud identity nor JWT is available |

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="JWT / OIDC">

JWT auth is the right choice for most GitHub Actions workflows, especially if
using GitHub-hosted runners or self-hosted runners that can access GitHub's OIDC
endpoint.

GitHub's OIDC token contains a rich set of claims that Vault can use as binding
conditions, including:

| Claim | Example value | Use for |
|---|---|---|
| `sub` | `repo:org/repo:environment:production/main` | Restrict to specific repository and branch |
| `repository` | `org/repo` | Restrict to specific repository |
| `environment` | `production` | Restrict to specific deployment environment |
| `ref` | `refs/heads/main` | Restrict to specific branch |
| `job_workflow_ref` | `org/repo/.github/workflows/deploy.yml` | Restrict to specific workflow file |

### Role configuration

The Vault role's `bound_claims` are where you translate these into access
controls. An unconstrained role that accepts any JWT from GitHub's OIDC
issuer is like giving every repository in your organization access to
those secrets. Use `bound_subject` for exact matches, or `bound_claims` with
`bound_claims_type: glob` for pattern matching.

```hcl
# Vault role — scoped to a specific repo and deployment environment
# bound_subject encodes: repo identity + environment context
vault write auth/jwt/role/github-actions-deploy \
  role_type=jwt \
  bound_audiences="https://github.com/your-org" \
  bound_subject="repo:your-org/your-repo:environment:production" \
  user_claim=repository \
  token_policies=deploy-production-policy \
  ttl=15m
```

The role binds Vault access to a specific repository and deployment environment.
Vault then issues a short-lived token only when the workflow presents the
expected GitHub identity.

### Workflow configuration

The workflow requires id-token: write permission to request a JWT from GitHub's
OIDC provider. Scope this to the specific job that needs Vault access rather
than the entire workflow — granting it at the workflow level gives every job the
ability to request OIDC tokens.

```yaml
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Scope permissions to this job only, not the whole workflow
    permissions:
      id-token: write   # Required: allows this job to request a GitHub OIDC token
      contents: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Retrieve secrets from Vault
        uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v3
        with:
          url: https://vault.example.com
          caCertificate: ${{ secrets.VAULT_CA_CERT }}
          method: jwt
          role: github-actions-deploy
          # jwtGitHubAudience customizes the aud claim — coordinate with Vault team
          # if you change this from the default
          secrets: |
            secret/data/ci/production/db password | DB_PASSWORD ;
            secret/data/ci/production/api key | API_KEY
```

The workflow requests a GitHub OIDC token only for the job that needs Vault
access. The Vault GitHub Action then exchanges that token for a Vault token and
retrieves only the secrets required for the deployment.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Cloud provider auth methods">

When self-hosted GitHub Actions runners execute on cloud infrastructure, the
instance already has a platform identity that Vault can validate against the
cloud provider's API. This avoids storing cloud credentials in GitHub just to
bootstrap Vault login.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="AWS">

Self-hosted runners on EC2 instances or ECS tasks with an IAM role attached can
authenticate to Vault using the AWS auth method. Vault calls AWS STS to validate
the instance or task role identity. No credential needs to be provisioned on the
runner.

### Role configuration

Bind the Vault role to the specific IAM role ARN attached to your runner
instances. The `iam` auth type is preferred over `ec2` — it works for both
EC2 and ECS and validates against the assumed-role ARN.

```hcl
# Vault role bound to the IAM role attached to GitHub Actions runner instances
vault write auth/aws/role/github-runner \
  auth_type=iam \
  bound_iam_principal_arn=arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/github-runner-role \
  token_policies=github-runner-policy \
  ttl=1h
```

The role binds Vault authentication to the IAM role attached to the runner.
Vault issues a token only when the runner proves that AWS identity.

### Workflow configuration

```yaml
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: self-hosted  # EC2 instance with IAM role attached
    steps:
      - name: Retrieve secrets via AWS IAM auth
        uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v3
        with:
          url: https://vault.example.com
          method: aws
          role: github-runner
          secrets: |
            secret/data/ci/build/registry password | REGISTRY_PASSWORD
```

The runner authenticates with its AWS identity — no credential needs to be
stored in GitHub. If Vault is configured with `iam_server_id_header_value`,
add the matching `awsIamServerId` input to the vault-action step.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Azure">

Self-hosted runners on Azure VMs or Azure Container Instances with a managed
identity assigned can authenticate to Vault using the Azure auth method. Vault
validates the identity against the Azure Instance Metadata Service (IMDS). No
credential needs to be provisioned on the runner.

### Role configuration

Bind the Vault role to the managed identity's service principal ID. This ensures
only runners with that specific managed identity can authenticate.

```hcl
# Vault role bound to the managed identity assigned to GitHub Actions runner VMs
vault write auth/azure/role/github-runner \
  token_policies=github-runner-policy \
  bound_subscription_ids=your-subscription-id \
  bound_resource_groups=your-resource-group \
  bound_service_principal_ids=your-managed-identity-object-id \
  ttl=1h
```

The role binds Vault authentication to the managed identity assigned to the
runner. Vault issues a token only when the runner presents the expected Azure
identity.

### Workflow configuration

```yaml
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: self-hosted  # Azure VM with managed identity assigned
    steps:
      - name: Retrieve secrets via Azure Managed Identity auth
        run: |
          AZURE_JWT=$(curl -s -H "Metadata: true" \
            "http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2018-02-01&resource=https://management.azure.com/" \
            | jq -r '.access_token')

          # bound_service_principal_ids is set on the role — no VM metadata required
          export VAULT_TOKEN=$(vault write -field=token auth/azure/login \
            role=github-runner \
            jwt=$AZURE_JWT)

          vault kv get -field=password secret/ci/build/registry \
            | echo "REGISTRY_PASSWORD=$(cat)" >> $GITHUB_ENV
```

The runner retrieves an Azure access token from IMDS, exchanges it for a Vault
token, and reads only the secret needed for the build.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="GCP">

Self-hosted runners on Google Compute Engine instances with a service account
attached can authenticate to Vault using the GCP auth method. Vault validates
the runner's identity against Google Cloud. Use an `iam` role type to bind
authentication to the attached service account identity. No credential needs to
be provisioned on the runner.

### Role configuration

Bind the Vault role to the specific GCP service account email attached to your
runner instances.

```hcl
# Vault role bound to the service account attached to GitHub Actions runner instances
vault write auth/gcp/role/github-runner \
  type=iam \
  token_policies=github-runner-policy \
  bound_service_accounts=github-runner@your-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com \
  ttl=1h
```

The role binds Vault authentication to the Google Cloud service account attached
to the runner. Vault issues a token only when the runner presents that GCP
identity.

### Workflow configuration

```yaml
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: self-hosted  # GCE instance with service account attached
    steps:
      - name: Retrieve secrets via GCP IAM auth
        run: |
          export VAULT_TOKEN=$(vault login -method=gcp -token-only \
            role=github-runner)

          vault kv get -field=password secret/ci/build/registry \
            | echo "REGISTRY_PASSWORD=$(cat)" >> $GITHUB_ENV
```

The runner logs in with its Google Cloud identity instead of using a bootstrap
credential stored in GitHub. The resulting Vault token is then used only for the
required secret read.

</Tab>
</Tabs>

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Kubernetes">

When self-hosted runners are managed by Actions Runner Controller on Kubernetes,
each runner pod has a projected service account token that Vault can validate
directly against the Kubernetes API. This is more authoritative than the GitHub
JWT in that environment and doesn't introduce a dependency on reaching GitHub's
OIDC endpoint from within the cluster.

### Role configuration

The Vault role binds to the runner's service account and namespace, following
the same pattern as any other Kubernetes workload.

```hcl
# Vault role for ARC-managed runner pods
vault write auth/kubernetes/role/github-runner \
  bound_service_account_names=arc-runner-sa \
  bound_service_account_namespaces=arc-runners \
  token_policies=github-runner-policy \
  ttl=1h
```

### Workflow configuration

The workflow doesn't need to request a JWT from GitHub's OIDC provider in this
case. Instead, the Vault GitHub Action can authenticate using the Kubernetes
auth method directly from the runner pod, which has the necessary service
account token mounted by Kubernetes.

```yaml
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: self-hosted  # ARC-managed runner on Kubernetes
    steps:
      - name: Retrieve secrets via Kubernetes auth
        uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v3
        with:
          url: https://vault.example.com
          method: kubernetes
          role: github-runner
          # kubernetesTokenPath defaults to the standard SA token path
          secrets: |
            secret/data/ci/build/registry password | REGISTRY_PASSWORD
```

The runner authenticates with the pod's projected service account token and
retrieves only the secret required for the build. This keeps the trust boundary
inside the Kubernetes cluster.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="AppRole">

AppRole is appropriate when self-hosted runners operate in air-gapped
environments where the GitHub OIDC endpoint
(token.actions.githubusercontent.com) is unreachable.

### Role configuration

The same response wrapping requirement applies here as elsewhere — delivering a
raw secret_id to a pipeline often turns into a manually distributed bootstrap
secret.

The secret_id should be issued by an external privileged process (an operator
workflow, a secrets provisioning system, or a Vault Agent on the controller) and
delivered to the runner as a single-use wrapping token. 

```hcl
# AppRole with tight constraints on secret_id lifecycle
vault write auth/approle/role/github-runner-airgapped \
  secret_id_ttl=10m \
  secret_id_num_uses=1 \
  token_ttl=1h \
  token_max_ttl=2h \
  token_policies=github-runner-policy
```

The AppRole configuration constrains bootstrap material to short-lived,
single-use delivery and limits the Vault token lifetime for the job.

### Workflow configuration

The workflow must include steps to unwrap the secret_id and authenticate with
Vault using the AppRole method.

```yaml
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: self-hosted  # Air-gapped runner
    steps:
      - name: Retrieve secrets via AppRole
        uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v3
        with:
          url: https://vault.example.com
          method: approle
          roleId: ${{ secrets.VAULT_ROLE_ID }}
          # wrapping token injected by external provisioning process
          # vault-action unwraps it automatically when wrapped=true
          secretId: ${{ secrets.VAULT_SECRET_ID_WRAPPING_TOKEN }}
          wrapped: true
          secrets: |
            secret/data/ci/build/registry password | REGISTRY_PASSWORD
```

The workflow unwraps the single-use bootstrap material and exchanges it for a
short-lived Vault token. The job then uses that token only for the secret reads
authorized by the AppRole policy.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="TLS Certificate">

TLS cert auth applies when self-hosted runners are long-lived, persistent build
agents rather than ephemeral pods.

### Role configuration

Vault validates the client certificate presented during the connection, with the
certificate's CN or SANs serving as
the identity binding. Certificate lifecycle management for these runners should
itself use Vault's PKI secrets engine to keep rotation automated.

```hcl
# Vault TLS cert role for a named runner fleet
vault write auth/cert/certs/github-runner-fleet \
  display_name=github-runner-fleet \
  certificate=@/path/to/runner-fleet-ca.crt \
  allowed_common_names="runner-*.build.example.com" \
  token_policies=github-runner-fleet-policy \
  ttl=4h
```

### Workflow configuration

The workflow authenticates to Vault using the client certificate provisioned on
the runner. The pattern assumes the runner's TLS cert is already managed and
rotated by Vault's PKI secrets engine. The workflow needs to reference the cert
and key from GitHub Secrets or another secure store.

```yaml
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: self-hosted  # Persistent runner with managed TLS cert
    steps:
      - name: Retrieve secrets via TLS cert auth
        uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v3
        with:
          url: https://vault.example.com
          method: cert
          # Certificate and key path provisioned on runner by Vault PKI engine
          clientKey: ${{ secrets.RUNNER_TLS_KEY }}
          clientCertificate: ${{ secrets.RUNNER_TLS_CERT }}
          secrets: |
            secret/data/ci/build/signing key | SIGNING_KEY
```

The runner authenticates with its client certificate and retrieves only the
secret needed for the signing workflow. This pattern fits persistent runners
that already participate in an mTLS-based operating model.

</Tab>
</Tabs>

## Policy

The Vault policy for any role should follow least privilege. A pipeline that
deploys to production should not be able to read staging secrets, and a staging
pipeline should not be able to read production secrets.

<VideoEmbed url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mmyvybxm30w"/>

An example Vault policy for a production deployment pipeline might look like this:

```hcl
# Vault policy: scope to exactly the paths this pipeline needs
path "secret/data/ci/production/db" {
  capabilities = ["read"]
}

# Deny access to everything else implicitly
```

The policy limits the workflow to the exact production secret path it needs.
Use separate policies for other environments so unrelated workflows do not
inherit broader access.

## Security considerations

There are several security considerations you need to align with your
organizational risk tolerance and operational constraints when designing your
GitHub Actions secrets management strategy:

- **JWT role constraints:** Bind Vault roles as tightly as possible with
  `bound_subject`, `bound_claims`, or both. A role that accepts any valid token
  from GitHub's OIDC issuer can allow unintended repositories or workflows to
  authenticate.
- **Job-level token permissions:** Grant `id-token: write` only to the job that
  needs Vault access. If you grant it at the workflow level, every job in that
  workflow can request an OIDC token.
- **Workflow-level scoping:** In repositories with multiple teams or deployment
  targets, bind roles to specific workflow files with `job_workflow_ref`.
  Without that restriction, any workflow in the repository with `id-token:
  write` may be able to authenticate to a repository-scoped role.

For a list of additional security considerations, refer to the [CI/CD security
considerations](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/security-considerations)
page.

## Integrate HCP Vault Radar

Before migrating to Vault-based secrets management, Vault Radar can scan
existing GitHub Actions workflow files and connected repositories for
credentials stored as hardcoded values. This is particularly important in
organizations that have accumulated years of workflows with credentials stored
across multiple GitHub organizations, environments, and secret scopes.

After migration, Vault Radar can monitor repositories continuously for new
secret introductions — catching cases where developers hardcode credentials that
should be going through Vault.

Refer to the [HCP Vault Radar quick start
tutorials](/hcp/tutorials/get-started-hcp-vault-radar) to learn about HCP Vault
Radar.

## Use Vault secrets sync for GitHub Secrets

Vault Secrets Sync provides an alternative solution for managing GitHub Actions
pipeline secrets that is useful in cases where using the GitHub OIDC provider is
not a feasible approach. Vault Secrets Sync simplifies configuration and lets you
to select specific secrets or secret paths for synchronization from Vault to
GitHub as repository or environment secrets.

When you use this approach you gain several benefits:

- Centralized policy and audit controls
- Repository-level access control
- No need to include authentication logic in your workflow
- Compatibility with all runner types without extra configuration
- Synchronization to GitHub when you rotate secrets in Vault
- Access to secrets through familiar GitHub Actions syntax without needing to
  learn Vault details

The Secrets Sync approach has the following limitations:

- Supports only static key/value (KV) Vault secrets engines.
- Copies secrets into GitHub instead of just-in-time access.
- Can require extra management of GitHub Personal Access Tokens or App Tokens
  for authentication.

The recommended approach, when possible, is to access Vault directly from your
GitHub Actions workflows using the Vault GitHub Action and GitHub's OIDC tokens.

Direct access provides the strongest security posture and the best secrets
management experience. Use Vault Secrets Sync for GitHub Secrets when the
direct integration is not feasible due to technical constraints or
organizational policies.

## Next steps

In this section of [managing CI/CD
secrets](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets),
you learned how to bind GitHub Actions workload identity to Vault auth methods
and how to scope secrets to specific repositories, environments, and workflow
files. Secure GitHub Actions secrets with HashiCorp Vault is part of the
[Secure systems pillar](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems).
